selection.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?

And how?","opinion":"Stereotype threat in STEM refers to the anxiety or fear of confirming negative stereotypes about one’s social group—such as women, racial minorities, or even older adults—when performing in science, technology, engineering, or mathematics fields. I’ve seen this in my own life, not directly in labs or coding sessions, but in how people react when I, a man from Bologna with only upper secondary education, speak about learning new things. There’s often a pause, a subtle disbelief—like, 'Can he really understand this?' It’s not about intelligence; it’s about the unspoken assumption that certain people don’t belong in intellectual spaces. I’ve noticed this especially when I travel and meet people from different backgrounds. In some places, older men like me are expected to be 'set in our ways,' but I’m still curious, still learning. That’s what stereotype threat does—it doesn’t just affect performance, it affects confidence, presence, and willingness to participate. Yes, we must address it. Because talent is everywhere, but opportunity is not. We can start by making classrooms and workplaces more inclusive—by highlighting diverse role models, encouraging collaborative learning, and avoiding comments that reinforce outdated norms. For example, when I serve at a café, I make sure to greet every customer with the same respect, regardless of age, accent, or clothing. That same principle applies in STEM: treat every learner as capable. I’ve seen how a simple shift in tone—like saying 'I believe you can figure this out' instead of 'This is hard, only smart people get it'—can change someone’s entire approach. It’s not about lowering standards; it’s about creating an environment where everyone feels they have a place. After all, in my years of working in Bologna, I’ve learned that the most valuable insights often come from those who’ve been overlooked. response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion: Stereotype threat in STEM refers to the anxiety or fear of confirming negative stereotypes about one’s social group—such as women, racial minorities, or even older adults—when performing in science, technology, engineering, or mathematics fields. I’ve seen this in my own life, not directly in labs or coding sessions, but in how people react when I, a man from Bologna with only upper secondary education, speak about learning new things. There’s often a pause, a subtle disbelief—like, 'Can he really understand this?' It’s not about intelligence; it’s about the unspoken assumption that certain people don’t belong in intellectual spaces. I’ve noticed this especially when I travel and meet people from different backgrounds. In some places, older men like me are expected to be 'set in our ways,' but I’m still curious, still learning. That’s what stereotype threat does—it doesn’t just affect performance, it affects confidence, presence, and willingness to participate. Yes, we must address it. Because talent is everywhere, but opportunity is not. We can start by making classrooms and workplaces more inclusive—by highlighting diverse role models, encouraging collaborative learning, and avoiding comments that reinforce outdated norms. For example, when I serve at a café, I make sure to greet every customer with the same respect, regardless of age, accent, or clothing. That same principle applies in STEM: treat every learner as capable. I’ve seen how a simple shift in tone—like saying 'I believe you can figure this out' instead of 'This is hard, only smart people get it'—can change someone’s entire approach. It’s not about lowering standards; it’s about creating an environment where everyone feels they have a place. After all, in my years of working in Bologna, I’ve learned that the most valuable insights often come from those who’ve been overlooked. We need more of that in science and tech.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I began by defining stereotype threat through personal and observed experiences, linking it to my own identity as a native-born Italian man with limited formal education. I reflected on how societal expectations shape perceptions, particularly regarding age and intellect. Drawing from my time in service work, I compared professional interactions—where respect is given regardless of background—to how similar principles should apply in STEM. I noted that anxiety stemming from stereotypes can hinder performance, even among capable individuals. I then argued that addressing this threat is essential not only for equity but for innovation, citing the value of diverse perspectives. My solution emphasized inclusive environments, mentorship, and language shifts, grounded in everyday human interactions I’ve witnessed. These insights were derived from my hobbies (travel, painting), my curiosity, and my lived experience in Bologna’s social fabric.
